title: API design — check what stuck
intro: Five checks on REST, idempotency, and evolvable contracts.

questions.prompt: Which pair of HTTP verbs is idempotent?
questions.explanation: PUT (replace) and DELETE (remove) are idempotent — repeating them lands the same state. GET is also idempotent (and safe), but POST creates a new resource each time, so it is not.

questions.prompt: Why does idempotency matter over an unreliable network?
choices: It lets a client safely retry after a dropped response without double-effects
It removes the need for versioning
questions.explanation: If the response is lost, the client retries. An idempotent call can be replayed safely; for a non-idempotent POST like a payment, you pass an idempotency key so the server dedupes. This is the API-level link to time-redundancy retries.

questions.prompt: The core difference between RPC and REST is…
choices: RPC is always faster and better
RPC exposes actions to call; REST exposes resources via a uniform interface
RPC only works inside a browser
questions.explanation: RPC is action-centric (call a remote function); REST is resource-centric (manipulate resources through uniform HTTP verbs). Each fits different contexts — REST for public/evolvable APIs, gRPC for fast internal calls.

choices: So the contract can evolve without breaking existing clients
To reduce the number of endpoints
questions.explanation: Clients depend on today's response shape. Versioning lets you introduce breaking changes on a new version while existing clients keep working against the old one — you can add freely, but you can't silently change.

questions.prompt: Where do a REST API's endpoints ultimately come from?
choices: The messages that cross the system boundary in the use-case sequence diagrams
A fixed list defined by the HTTP spec
questions.explanation: Endpoints are derived, not guessed: every message crossing the system boundary in a sequence diagram becomes an endpoint. Grouping those messages by the resource they act on yields the resource model. API design is a translation of the requirements you already captured.
